text:
Centre for Epilepsy

Neurosciences Division 

Clinic Date: 20 November 2015 

Dr A. Rahman 
Consultant Neurologist  
Centre for Epilepsy  
King’s College Hospital, London  

Dear Dr A. Rahman, 

Mr Thomas Green, DOB: 04-09-2003, Hospital No: K847129 NHS No. 9384756102  
Flat 12, 27 Campus Road, London, SE5 9AB  

Diagnosis: 

Focal epilepsy with stress- and sleep-loss-related breakthrough events; improved consistency on Lamotrigine; Levetiracetam previously associated with irritability and discontinued

Aetiology: 

No structural lesion identified on MRI; normal perinatal and developmental history

Other Medical Problems: 

Intermittent tension-type headache; myopia corrected with lenses

Management Plan: 

Continue Lamotrigine (single brand) 200 mg twice daily. Maintain a seizure and sleep diary. I have advised timing doses strictly 12 hours apart and taking evening dose before prolonged gaming sessions. We will arrange a 24-hour ambulatory EEG if events persist beyond next review. Review in four months; check trough Lamotrigine level at that visit.

This patient is the lead of a university esports club with prolonged evening screen time and irregular sleep during tournament weeks. He reports brief focal aware seizures characterised by a rising epigastric sensation, left-hand paraesthesias, and transient word-finding difficulty lasting 30–60 seconds without loss of awareness or postictal confusion. Seizure days: six/30 this month, clustering after late-night practice sessions extending past 01:00 and on days following <5 hours of sleep. There have been no convulsions, no injuries, and no episodes while driving; he is not currently driving. Caffeine intake is moderate (2–3 energy drinks on event days). Alcohol minimal. No missed doses reported in the past month; he previously tried Levetiracetam but stopped due to mood change. We discussed non-prescriptive strategies he reports finding acceptable, including scheduling earlier scrims, using blue-light filters, and setting a device curfew to preserve sleep. No other medication changes today.

Yours sincerely

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states: "Seizure days: six/30 this month". This indicates seizures occurred on 6 days in the past 30 days. No other numeric seizure frequency is provided that is higher. There is mention of clustering after late-night sessions, but no number of seizures per cluster is given. Therefore, the best normalized frequency is 6 per month, interpreting 'six/30 this month' as 6 in a month.
